description: The provided image is an invalid placeholder displaying only a "Check for updates" icon, with no relevant material science research content visible. No information related to the studied austenitic Fe30Mn6.5Al0.3C low-density steel, hydrogen-induced microband formation, microstructural evolution, or mechanical performance can be extracted from this erroneous image. Associated paper text reports that 101 mass ppm hydrogen introduction promotes microband and deformation band formation in the alloy, refines dislocation cell size, increases misorientation angles of geometric necessary boundaries, enhances strain hardening capability, and improves both strength and ductility without hydrogen embrittlement occurrence, leading to excellent hydrogen resistance comparable to other high-performance face-centered cubic alloys.

This figure presents the hydrogen thermal desorption analysis (TDA) curve of a hydrogen-charged austenitic Fe30Mn6.5Al0.3C low-density steel sample. The x-axis represents temperature ranging from 300 K to 1000 K, and the y-axis denotes H desorption rate in unit of mass ppm/min. The curve shows a single distinct desorption peak centered at approximately 720 K, which corresponds to the diffusion of lattice solute hydrogen. The total measured hydrogen concentration of the tested sample is 101 mass ppm. The sample was pre-charged with 100 MPa high-pressure gaseous hydrogen at 543 K for 200 h to obtain uniform hydrogen distribution, and the TDA test was performed at a heating rate of 100 K/h. This result confirms the absorbed hydrogen exists mainly as lattice solute hydrogen, and verifies the hydrogen content remains nearly constant during subsequent tensile tests, providing quantitative hydrogen content support for studies on hydrogen effects on the steel's mechanical performance and microstructure.

This figure consists of two subplots presenting tensile mechanical properties of uncharged and hydrogen-charged (101 mass ppm hydrogen content) austenitic Fe30Mn6.5Al0.3C (wt.%) low-density steel. Subplot (a) shows engineering stress-strain curves, while subplot (b) shows true stress-strain curves and corresponding strain-hardening (SH) curves, all obtained from tensile tests conducted at 295 K in laboratory air with an initial strain rate of 5.0×10^-5 s^-1. The hydrogen-charged sample exhibits slightly higher yield strength, ultimate tensile strength and elongation to fracture than the uncharged sample. Both samples show nearly identical strain-hardening behavior with four distinguishable stages (A, B, C, D), and no extra hardening stage associated with deformation twinning is observed for the hydrogen-charged sample. The results demonstrate that hydrogen does not degrade the tensile performance of the studied steel, indicating good hydrogen embrittlement resistance of the alloy.

This figure displays two inverse pole figures along the tensile axis direction of tensile-deformed austenitic Fe30Mn6.5Al0.3C low-density steel. Subfigure (a) covers the uncharged sample with hydrogen content < 5 mass ppm, and subfigure (b) covers the high-pressure hydrogen-charged sample with 101 mass ppm hydrogen. Measured grain orientations are classified into four types corresponding to different dislocation structures: Type 1a (black points, near <101>//tensile axis, cell block structure with primary slip), Type 1b (gray points, along [112]-[101] line, cell block structure with two-fold slip), Type 2 (red points, near <001>//tensile axis, dislocation cell structure), Type 3 (blue points, near <111>//tensile axis, complex dislocation structure with microbands), each marked with Miller indices. Slight shifts of orientation distribution for Type 1a, 1b and 3 grains are observed after hydrogen charging. This figure links grain orientation to deformation dislocation structure type, supporting analysis of hydrogen effects on orientation-dependent deformation behavior of the steel.

This set of micrographs presents the classification of dislocation structures in uncharged austenitic Fe30Mn6.5Al0.3C low-density steel after tensile deformation. Subfigures (a, b) correspond to Type 1a dislocation structure: (a) is the low-magnification view with a 1 μm scale bar, showing nearly parallel geometrically necessary boundaries (GNBs) aligned along {111} slip planes relative to the tensile axis (TA); (b) is the high-magnification view with a 200 nm scale bar, revealing incidental dislocation boundaries (IDBs) inside GNB-bounded regions. Subfigures (c, d) correspond to Type 1b dislocation structure: (c) is the low-magnification view with a 1 μm scale bar, showing two sets of intersecting GNBs (GNB1, GNB2); (d) is the high-magnification view with a 500 nm scale bar, displaying finer secondary GNB2s inside GNB1 domains. This figure provides the baseline microstructure of uncharged samples, supporting analysis of the hydrogen-induced microbanding mechanism and hydrogen embrittlement resistance of the alloy.

This set of scanning electron microscopy (SEM, electron channeling contrast imaging mode) micrographs classifies deformation-induced dislocation structures in uncharged austenitic Fe30Mn6.5Al0.3C low-density steel after room-temperature tensile testing. Subfigures (a) (scale bar 1 μm) and (b) (scale bar 200 nm) present Type 2 dislocation structure: homogeneously distributed ~200 nm sized dislocation cells (DCs), formed in grains oriented close to [0 0 1] parallel to the tensile axis (TA) with octahedral slip activation. Subfigures (c)-(e) (scale bar 200 nm for d, 250 nm for e, c scale partially unreadable) show Type 3 complex dislocation structure, formed in grains oriented close to [1 1 1] parallel to TA, consisting of uniform DCs, inhomogeneous 150-200 nm wide plate-shaped microbands (MBs) along high-stress slip planes, and deformation twins (DTs). This figure verifies that dislocation structure types are determined by grain orientation relative to loading direction, supporting subsequent analysis of hydrogen effects on deformation microstructures.

This figure presents combined electron channeling contrast imaging (ECCI) and electron backscatter diffraction (EBSD) analysis of geometrically necessary boundary (GNB) and incidental dislocation boundary (IDB) misorientations in Type 1a cell blocks (CBs) of austenitic Fe30Mn6.5Al0.3C low-density steel. Subfigure (a) is an ECC image with the {111} crystal direction marked, CB1-CB5 regions indicated, and a 1 μm scale bar. Subfigure (b) is a grain reference orientation deviation EBSD map, with color coding representing misorientation from 0° to 7°, measured point misorientation values labeled, and a 1 μm scale bar. Subfigure (c) is a magnified ECC image of the CB1 interior, marking GNB1 and IDB1-IDB5 with a 200 nm scale bar. Subfigure (d) is the point-to-point misorientation profile along the CB1 interior, showing misorientation angles of IDB1 to IDB5 are 1.0°, 0.85°, 0.5°, 0.7°, and 0.6° respectively. The figure provides quantitative experimental evidence for misorientation characterization of dislocation boundaries in deformed CB structures, supporting analysis of hydrogen effects on deformation microstructures of the studied steel.

This set of 4 line plots (a-d) presents misorientation distribution statistics of geometrically necessary boundaries (GNBs) and incidental dislocation boundaries (IDBs) in uncharged and 101 mass ppm hydrogen-charged austenitic Fe30Mn6.5Al0.3C low-density steel. Subplot (a) shows GNB1 misorientation in Type 1a cell blocks, (b) shows IDB misorientation in Type 1a cell blocks, (c) shows GNB1 misorientation in Type 1b cell blocks, (d) shows GNB2 misorientation in Type 1b cell blocks. Red curves represent uncharged samples, blue curves represent hydrogen-charged samples. The average misorientation increases after hydrogen charging: Type 1a GNB1 rises from 1.62° to 2.70°, IDB rises from 1.14° to 1.38°, Type 1b GNB1 rises from 2.64° to 3.65°, Type 1b GNB2 rises from 2.67° to 3.71°, indicating hydrogen promotes GNB misorientation increment, which supports the GNB-assisted microband nucleation mechanism and verifies the hydrogen-enhanced microbanding effect that improves hydrogen embrittlement resistance of the tested steel.

This set of figures presents combined electron channeling contrast imaging (ECCI) and electron backscatter diffraction (EBSD) characterization of cell block (CB) structures in tensile deformed austenitic Fe30Mn6.5Al0.3C low-density steel. Subfigure (a) is a low-magnification ECC image of Type 1a CB structure with 1 μm scale bar, showing parallel geometrically necessary boundary (GNB1) arrangement, with tensile axis (TA) marked. Subfigure (b) is the high-magnification ECC image of the framed region in (a) with 500 nm scale bar, measuring dense dislocation wall DDW1a thickness of 88 nm, microbands MB1a and MB1b thickness of 150 nm and 190 nm respectively. Subfigure (c) is the corresponding KAM-EBSD map of (b) with 500 nm scale bar, giving misorientation angles of 1.0±0.1° for DDW1a, 1.9±0.1° for MB1a, 2.0±0.2° for MB1b, confirming that misorientation <2° corresponds to DDWs while >2° corresponds to MBs. Subfigure (d) is ECC image of Type 1b CB structure with 500 nm scale bar, showing localized MB1a (150 nm thick) and MB1b (170 nm thick) formation along GNB1 sections. This figure verifies MB nucleation along GNBs, supporting the hydrogen-enhanced microbanding mechanism in the studied steel.

This figure presents microstructural characterization of tensile-deformed austenitic Fe30Mn6.5Al0.3C low-density steel. Subfigure (a) is a bright-field scanning transmission electron microscopy (BF-STEM) image with a 200 nm scale bar, showing a microband (MB) nucleated along primary geometrically necessary boundary (GNB) in a Type 1 cell block, with tensile axis (TA) and {111} crystallographic direction marked. The image was acquired under two-beam condition using a {111} diffraction vector, from a grain oriented close to [10 3 16]//TA. Subfigure (b) is the corresponding selected area electron diffraction pattern with a 20 1/nm scale bar, collected along the [101] zone axis, with diffraction spots including 022, 111, 200, -111, -200, 1-1-1 indexed. No extra diffraction spots are observed, confirming no ordered κ-carbide formation in the tested steel. This figure verifies the GNB-assisted MB nucleation mechanism, supporting the conclusion of excellent hydrogen embrittlement resistance of the studied steel.

This figure characterizes the deformation microstructure of hydrogen-charged austenitic Fe30Mn6.5Al0.3C low-density steel after room temperature tensile testing, investigating hydrogen's effect on Type 2 dislocation structure. Subfigure (a) is an inverse pole figure (IPF) EBSD map with 10 μm scale bar, showing macroscopic deformation bands (DB1, DB2, DB3) formed in a grain oriented close to [1 1 18] parallel to the tensile axis (TA). Subfigure (b) is the magnified EBSD map of the marked area in (a) with 5 μm scale bar, focusing on DB1 with orientation [2 1 15]//TA, with <110>{111} slip system marked. Subfigures (c-e) are electron channeling contrast imaging (ECCI) micrographs with 1 μm, 500 nm, 250 nm scale bars respectively, revealing fine dislocation structures inside DB1: microbands (MBs, 150-200 nm thick, spaced 2-3 μm along {111} slip planes) and equiaxed dislocation cells (DCs). This figure confirms hydrogen promotes multi-scale strain localization, forming macroscopic DBs and microscopic MBs/DCs, supporting the conclusion that hydrogen-enhanced microbanding improves strain hardening and hydrogen embrittlement resistance of the studied steel.

This chart plots the statistical size distribution of dislocation cells ($D_{DC}$) in type 2 dislocation structures of austenitic Fe30Mn6.5Al0.3C low-density steel, measured from electron channeling contrast (ECC) images. The blue curve corresponds to the hydrogen-charged sample with 101 mass ppm hydrogen content, while the red curve corresponds to the uncharged sample with <5 mass ppm hydrogen content. The x-axis presents dislocation cell size in nm, and the y-axis presents the fraction of cells with the corresponding size. The average dislocation cell size is $220\pm10$ nm for the uncharged sample, and is refined to $192\pm10$ nm after hydrogen charging, indicating that hydrogen induces dislocation cell size refinement in the tested steel, which is consistent with previous observations in face-centered cubic metals. This figure supports the conclusion that hydrogen modifies deformation dislocation structures in this low-density steel.

This set of 4 electron channeling contrast (ECC, a scanning electron microscopy based technique) images present hydrogen-influenced Type 3 dislocation structures in grains oriented close to [5 4 6]//tensile axis (TA) of austenitic Fe30Mn6.5Al0.3C low-density steel. Subfigure (a, 500 nm scale bar) shows the base dislocation structure consisting of dense dislocation walls (DDWs) and dislocation cells (DCs, average size ~210 nm). Subfigure (b, 500 nm scale bar) displays deformation bands (DBs, 0.5–1.0 μm thickness) propagating through the DDW/DC structure. Subfigure (c, 500 nm scale bar) exhibits both DBs and microbands (MBs, 2–3 μm spacing, ~200 nm thickness) cutting across the dislocation structure. Subfigure (d, 200 nm scale bar) gives a detailed view of an MB with bright contrast under (2 0 2) channeling conditions. DBs and MBs align along (-111) plane traces, corresponding to secondary slip system activation. This figure confirms hydrogen promotes DDW, DB and MB formation, supports the DB-assisted MB nucleation mechanism, and correlates to the hydrogen-enhanced microbanding effect that improves the steel's hydrogen embrittlement resistance.

This set of bright-field scanning transmission electron microscopy (BF-STEM, a class of TEM) images displays dislocation structures in tensile-deformed austenitic Fe30Mn6.5Al0.3C low-density steel. Subfigure (a) shows the structure in a grain with orientation ~[6 5 8]//tensile axis (TA) from the uncharged sample (hydrogen content <5 mass ppm), while subfigure (b) shows the structure in a grain with orientation ~[9 7 12]//TA from the high-pressure gaseous hydrogen-charged sample (hydrogen content 101 mass ppm). Both images were acquired under two-beam condition using a (111)-type diffraction vector, with 1 μm scale bars. Visible microstructural features include dense dislocation walls (DDWs), cell blocks (CB), and deformation bands (DBs, marked DB1, DB2, DB3), where DBs shear DDW segments as indicated by arrows. The hydrogen-charged sample exhibits larger shear offsets (~75–100 nm) at DB-DDW intersections, coarser DBs (thickness ~0.5–1.0 μm, vs ~0.3–0.5 μm for the uncharged sample), and merged DB structures, confirming hydrogen-enhanced strain localization and microband coarsening, which supports the hydrogen-enhanced microbanding mechanism linked to the steel's excellent hydrogen embrittlement resistance.

This figure presents characterization results of hydrogen-charged austenitic Fe30Mn6.5Al0.3C low-density steel. Subfigure (a) is a bright-field scanning transmission electron microscopy (BF-STEM) image with a 250 nm scale bar, showing that a deformation band (DB) shears the dense dislocation wall (DDW)/dislocation cell (DC) structure in a grain oriented close to [9 7 12]//tensile axis (TA) direction; sheared DC walls are marked by white arrows, and the image was acquired under two-beam condition using a (1 1 1)-type diffraction vector. Subfigure (b) is the corresponding selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern along the [1 0 1] zone axis with a 20 1/nm scale bar, indexed with diffraction spots of {111}, {200}, {022} planes and their equivalent indices, with no extra diffraction spots observed. This finding confirms strain localization within DBs arises from hydrogen-induced plasticity effect, supporting the conclusion that hydrogen-enhanced microbanding improves the hydrogen embrittlement resistance of the studied steel.

This set of 4 bright-field scanning transmission electron microscopy (BF-STEM) images characterize the dislocation structures inside deformation bands (DBs) in hydrogen-charged Fe30Mn6.5Al0.3C low-density austenitic steel after tensile testing, acquired under two-beam condition with a (111) diffraction vector. Subfigure (a) has a scale bar of 250 nm, while (b), (c), (d) have 100 nm scale bars. Multiple dislocation configurations are labeled: A for groups of closely spaced screw dislocations, DDW for dense dislocation walls, D for dissolved DDW segments, B for DDWs, C for paired screw dislocation configurations, J for dislocation cusps with jogs, CS for cross-slipped and double-cross slipped dislocations. A high dislocation density of ~1-3×10^14 m⁻² is observed within DBs, together with active dislocation cross-slip, DDW dissolution, and dislocation interactions forming Lomer-Cottrell locks. The observations confirm intense hydrogen-promoted plastic activity inside DBs, supporting the hydrogen-enhanced microbanding (HEMB) mechanism: cross-slip and massive glide of dislocation groups shear pre-existing dislocation structures, facilitating microband nucleation and plastic relaxation, thus improving the hydrogen embrittlement resistance of the steel.

This figure displays the effect of hydrogen charging on the distributions of cell boundary misorientation ($\theta_{DC}$) and dislocation cell size ($D_{DC}$) for Type 3 dislocation structures in austenitic Fe30Mn6.5Al0.3C low-density steel. Subplot (a) shows the fraction of misorientation (x-axis: 0-2 deg) for uncharged and hydrogen-charged samples: the average $\bar{\theta}_{DC}$ is 0.87° for uncharged samples, and slightly increases to 0.90° for hydrogen-charged samples. Subplot (b) presents the fraction of dislocation cell size (x-axis: 0-500 nm): the average $\bar{D}_{DC}$ is $236\pm10$ nm for uncharged samples, and decreases to $212\pm10$ nm after hydrogen charging. These results indicate that hydrogen induces a slight increase of cell boundary misorientation and obvious refinement of dislocation cell size, supporting that hydrogen alters deformation dislocation structures, consistent with the hydrogen-enhanced microbanding mechanism in the studied steel.

This group of SEM fractographs shows the tensile fracture surfaces of Fe30Mn6.5Al0.3C austenitic low-density steel, including uncharged (a-c) and hydrogen-charged (d-f) samples. Subfigures (a) and (d) are low-magnification views with a 100 μm scale bar, while (b,c) are high-magnification views of the framed regions in (a), and (e,f) are high-magnification views of the framed regions in (d), all with 20 μm scale bars. Both sample groups exhibit typical ductile dimple fracture morphology with no intergranular cracking observed. The hydrogen-charged sample presents obviously refined dimple size compared with the uncharged sample, indicating hydrogen does not alter the ductile fracture nature of the steel. These fracture characteristics confirm that hydrogen-induced microbanding relieves strain localization damage, supporting the core conclusion that the studied FeMnAlC steel has excellent hydrogen embrittlement resistance.

This figure is a schematic illustration of hydrogen-induced microband (MB) nucleation mechanisms in austenitic Fe30Mn6.5Al0.3C low-density steel. Subfigure (a) depicts the Geometrically Necessary Boundary (GNB)-assisted MB nucleation mechanism: hydrogen induces GNB splitting, and shear localization along GNBs (with incidental dislocation boundaries, IDBs) promotes MB nucleation. Hydrogen charging increases GNB misorientation (2~8°) compared to uncharged samples (2~3°), caused by enhanced storage of geometrically necessary dislocations under hydrogen-induced localized shear. Subfigure (b) illustrates the Deformation Band (DB)-assisted MB nucleation mechanism: hydrogen induces strain localization inside DBs, where secondary slip systems shear the primary dislocation structure (dislocation cells, DCs; dense dislocation walls, DDWs) to form MB via localized shear. These two MB nucleation mechanisms contribute to internal stress relaxation of deformation structures, alleviate hydrogen-induced strain localization damage, and improve the hydrogen embrittlement resistance of the studied steel.

This figure plots the index of hydrogen embrittlement (IHE) against ultimate tensile strength (UTS, unit: MPa) for multiple austenitic steels and face-centered cubic multi-component alloys tested under high-pressure gaseous hydrogen charging conditions. The top color bar indicates hydrogen embrittlement (HE) resistance, increasing from low (red, left, IHE>0.1) to high (blue, right, IHE<0). Four fracture modes are marked with different symbols: SD/D (small-sized dimple/dimple fracture, blue dots), D+IG (ductile + intergranular fracture, light blue dots), HIS (hydrogen-induced successive slip/quasi-cleavage, yellow dots), IG/TG+QC (intergranular/transgranular + quasi-cleavage, red dots). Tested materials include FeMnC TWIP steels, 304, 316, 317L, 316L, CoCrFeMnNi, FeNiCrCo, HP160, CoNiV, 310S, and the studied Fe30Mn6.5Al0.3C low-density steel. The studied steel lies in the high HE resistance region with IHE ~ -0.04 and UTS ~ 580 MPa, comparable to other high HE-resistant fcc alloys, confirming its excellent HE resistance derived from hydrogen-enhanced plasticity accommodating effects.
